subject: U.S. Telecom & Space Infrastructure
executive_summary:
The U.S. telecommunications and space sectors are navigating a period of unprecedented threat intensity coupled with massive strategic investment. The Salt Typhoon campaign, described as the worst telecom hack in American history, compromised at least nine major U.S. telecommunications companies including AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile, and Lumen, with the Chinese state-linked group accessing CALEA wiretapping systems used by law enforcement and intelligence agencies. The hackers obtained metadata from over a million users concentrated in the Washington D.C. area, including date and time stamps, source and destination IP addresses, and phone numbers. By August 2025, the FBI stated Salt Typhoon had breached at least 200 companies across 80 countries. Despite carrier claims of eviction, experts testifying before the Senate Commerce Committee in December 2025 agreed that U.S. communications networks remain vulnerable, with unpatched routers (some with security patches available for seven years), weak password protocols, and fundamental hygiene failures enabling the breach.

The space domain has become a central front in great power competition. China's operational satellite fleet exceeded 1,060 by mid-2025, with hundreds dedicated to ISR, while both China and Russia continue advancing counterspace weapons including anti-satellite kinetic kill vehicles, directed-energy weapons, and electronic warfare capabilities. U.S. space assets face daily or near-daily attacks including signal jamming, laser dazzling, and cyberattacks. GPS jamming and spoofing has become a persistent global problem, with Russia-linked interference disrupting the Baltic Sea and Middle East, and China developing similar capabilities. Russia is developing a nuclear-powered electronic warfare satellite that could jam GPS signals across a significant portion of the globe. The CSIS Space Threat Assessment 2025 noted that Chinese and Russian satellites continue displaying increasingly advanced maneuvering capabilities in both LEO and GEO.

Subsea cable infrastructure represents another critical vulnerability, with over 95% of data and $10 trillion in daily financial transactions traveling across 1.5 million kilometers of submarine cables. International investigators have accused both China and Russia of deliberately cutting undersea cables, with landing stations often situated in isolated coastal areas with limited security. The FCC adopted new measures restricting foreign involvement in cable projects, and the Strategic Subsea Cables Act of 2025 was introduced to bolster punishments for sabotage.

On the investment side, the response has been substantial. FY 2026 Space Force funding approaches $40 billion, with a nearly 40% increase including the reconciliation act's $13.8 billion supplement. The Space Development Agency awarded $3.5 billion for 72 Tranche 3 tracking layer satellites, while $190 million was allocated for proliferated LEO satcom services. SpaceX secured roughly $6 billion in military launch contracts extending into the 2030s, with its Starshield program providing encrypted, anti-jam satellite services purpose-built for national security. The Space Force is transitioning from a support role to an active warfighting posture, with Chief of Space Operations Gen. Saltzman declaring the service will pursue space superiority as the foundation for dominance across all military domains.

key_findings.headline: Salt Typhoon: Worst Telecom Hack in American History
key_findings.detail: Chinese state-linked group Salt Typhoon compromised at least nine major U.S. telecom companies (AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile, Spectrum, Lumen, Consolidated Communications, Windstream) and accessed CALEA wiretapping systems used by law enforcement. Metadata from over one million users in the D.C. area was exfiltrated. By August 2025, the FBI reported the group had breached 200+ companies across 80 countries. The FBI announced a $10 million bounty in April 2025, and the Treasury sanctioned Sichuan Juxinhe Network Technology for direct involvement. Despite carrier claims of containment, Senate testimony in December 2025 confirmed vulnerabilities remain actively exploited.

key_findings.headline: Fundamental Cybersecurity Failures Across Major Carriers
key_findings.detail: Expert testimony before the Senate Commerce Committee revealed that the Salt Typhoon breach was enabled by 'rudimentary' cybersecurity failures: routers with available patches from seven years prior never applied, weak password protocols allowing unauthorized credential acquisition, and absence of basic two-factor authentication. AT&T and Verizon failed to respond to congressional requests for remediation documentation, and in June 2025 Senator Cantwell demanded documentation proving remediation of ongoing vulnerabilities. The FCC rolled back protective rules in November 2025, shifting to voluntary 'collaboration' with carriers.

key_findings.headline: China's Counterspace Capabilities Expanding Rapidly
key_findings.detail: China's operational satellite fleet exceeded 1,060 by mid-2025 with hundreds dedicated to ISR. Both China and Russia continue advancing anti-satellite kinetic kill vehicles, directed-energy weapons, and electronic warfare capabilities. U.S. space assets face daily or near-daily attacks including signal jamming, laser dazzling, and cyberattacks. Russia is developing a nuclear-powered electronic warfare satellite capable of jamming GPS signals across a significant portion of the globe. The CSIS Space Threat Assessment 2025 documented increasingly advanced maneuvering capabilities by Chinese and Russian satellites in both LEO and GEO.

key_findings.headline: GPS Jamming and Spoofing Becoming Persistent Global Threat
key_findings.detail: GPS jamming and spoofing has escalated from isolated incidents to persistent features of modern conflict. Russia-linked electronic warfare has caused widespread GNSS interference across the Baltic Sea, forcing aircraft diversions. Over 1,100 commercial ships experienced navigation disruption in a single incident in UAE, Qatari, Omani and Iranian waters. Nations including China, Russia, and Iran have weaponized GPS signal weaknesses. America's digital economy and military logistics are deeply dependent on GPS timing and positioning signals with limited backup alternatives.

key_findings.headline: Submarine Cable Infrastructure Vulnerable to State-Sponsored Sabotage
key_findings.detail: Over 95% of data and $10 trillion in daily financial transactions travel across 1.5 million km of submarine cables. International investigators have accused China and Russia of deliberately cutting undersea cables, with China linked to ships cutting cables near Taiwan and Russia blamed for Baltic Sea incidents. Cable landing stations are often situated in isolated coastal areas with limited physical and cybersecurity protections. The Strategic Subsea Cables Act of 2025 was introduced to bolster punishments and authorize sanctions for foreign individuals who intentionally damage cables.

key_findings.headline: Space Force Budget Surges to Nearly $40 Billion
key_findings.detail: FY 2026 space funding approaches $40 billion including the base budget request of $26.3 billion plus $13.8 billion from the reconciliation act -- a nearly 40% increase from FY 2025. The Space Development Agency awarded $3.5 billion for 72 Tranche 3 tracking layer satellites for the Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture. $190 million was allocated for proliferated LEO satcom services. The Space Force is transitioning from a support role to an active warfighting posture under the Golden Dome initiative for boost-phase missile defense.

key_findings.headline: SpaceX Dominates National Security Launch with $6 Billion in Contracts
key_findings.detail: SpaceX secured approximately $6 billion in Pentagon launch contracts extending well into the 2030s, receiving roughly 60% of Space Force missions (28 of 54 launches across five order years). SpaceX's Starshield program provides encrypted, anti-jam satellite services purpose-built for military and national security use, with potential deployment on U.S. Air Force gunships. The $13.7 billion NSSL Phase 3 Lane 1 contracts were split between Blue Origin, SpaceX, and ULA.

recent_developments.headline: Senate Hearing Confirms U.S. Telecom Networks Remain Vulnerable
recent_developments.summary: The Senate Commerce Committee held a hearing where current and former government cybersecurity experts agreed that U.S. communications networks remain vulnerable despite claims by AT&T and Verizon that they had evicted Salt Typhoon. Witnesses cited unpatched equipment, weak authentication, and the FCC's rollback of protective rules in November 2025 as ongoing concerns.
recent_developments.impact: Undermined carrier assurances of remediation; increased pressure for mandatory cybersecurity standards for telecom providers.

recent_developments.headline: FCC Moves to Expel Hong Kong Telecom from U.S. Networks
recent_developments.summary: The Federal Communications Commission took action to remove Hong Kong Telecom from U.S. telecommunications networks, citing national security concerns about Chinese government influence over network equipment and services operating within the United States.
recent_developments.impact: Part of broader effort to remove Chinese-linked entities from critical telecommunications infrastructure; follows existing covered equipment list under Section 2 of the Secure Networks Act.

recent_developments.headline: Space Development Agency Awards $3.5 Billion for 72 Tracking Satellites
recent_developments.summary: The SDA awarded firm-fixed-price contracts to L3Harris, Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, and Rocket Lab for 72 Tranche 3 tracking layer satellites for the Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture in LEO, with launches beginning in 2029. This represents the largest single investment in the proliferated space architecture to date.
recent_developments.impact: Accelerates the shift toward resilient, proliferated satellite architectures less vulnerable to single-point-of-failure attacks.

recent_developments.headline: Space Force Awards $13.7 Billion in National Security Launch Contracts
recent_developments.summary: The U.S. Space Force awarded $13.7 billion in NSSL Phase 3 Lane 1 contracts split between Blue Origin, SpaceX, and ULA, securing national security launch capability through the 2030s. SpaceX received approximately 60% of the 54 planned missions.
recent_developments.impact: Ensured assured access to space for national security payloads while maintaining competitive launch provider base.

recent_developments.headline: White House Executive Order on 'Ensuring American Space Superiority'
recent_developments.summary: The White House released an executive order directing agencies to update strategies for space operations, requiring Commerce Department reporting on spectrum management opportunities within 120 days and streamlining regulatory processes for satellite operations and earth station siting.
recent_developments.impact: Signaled whole-of-government prioritization of space superiority; streamlined regulatory framework for commercial space operations supporting national security.

risk_assessment.current_risk: The U.S. telecom and space sectors face critical-level risk from the combination of proven, large-scale Chinese cyber penetration of telecommunications networks (Salt Typhoon affecting 9+ carriers and 200+ companies globally), escalating counterspace threats from China and Russia (daily jamming, directed-energy, and kinetic capabilities), vulnerable submarine cable infrastructure ($10 trillion/day in transactions at risk), and persistent GPS jamming/spoofing that has become a normalized feature of modern conflict. While the space investment response has been substantial (~$40B for FY2026), telecom cybersecurity governance has actually weakened with the FCC's November 2025 rollback of protective rules.
key_drivers: Salt Typhoon penetration of 9+ U.S. telecom carriers with CALEA wiretapping system access, potentially still active
China's satellite fleet exceeding 1,060 with advancing counterspace weapons including anti-satellite and electronic warfare
Russia developing nuclear-powered electronic warfare satellite for global GPS disruption capability
Persistent GPS jamming and spoofing becoming normalized across conflict zones with over 10,000 incidents annually
Submarine cable sabotage emerging as a hybrid warfare tactic by China and Russia
FCC rollback of telecom cybersecurity protections in November 2025 weakening carrier accountability
Carrier failure to demonstrate full remediation of Salt Typhoon intrusions despite congressional demands
mitigation_actions: FY 2026 Space Force budget approaching $40 billion with 40% increase including reconciliation funding
SDA Tranche 3 tracking layer: $3.5 billion for 72 proliferated LEO satellites reducing single-point failures
NSSL Phase 3: $13.7 billion in launch contracts ensuring assured space access through 2030s
FBI $10 million bounty and Treasury sanctions against Salt Typhoon-linked entities
FCC expelled Hong Kong Telecom and adopted submarine cable security measures
Strategic Subsea Cables Act of 2025 introduced with sabotage punishments and sanctions authority
SpaceX Starshield providing encrypted, anti-jam military satellite communications
Commercial Augmentation Space Reserve targeting 20 wartime satcom contracts by end of 2026
